text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Part 13: The Final Challenge — Trainer Red

### Mt. Silver
152. After obtaining all 16 badges, Mt. Silver entrance opens (Route 28)
153. Multi-floor cave dungeon with high-level wild Pokemon (Larvitar, Ursaring, Sneasel, Misdreavus, Pupitar)

### Trainer Red
154. Summit of Mt. Silver
155. Red says nothing — "..." — and battle begins
156. **Red's Team**:
    - Pikachu Lv81
    - Espeon Lv73
    - Snorlax Lv75
    - Venusaur Lv77
    - Charizard Lv77
    - Blastoise Lv77

name: Overview
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Overview

The Ruins of Alph is a mysterious archaeological site south of Violet City and west of Route 32 in Johto. It contains:
- 4 Puzzle Chambers (each with a sliding puzzle)
- 1 Inner Chamber (main hall with statues)
- 4 Hidden Item Rooms (accessed via secret passages)
- 4 Word Rooms (Unown text chambers)
- 1 Research Center
- Unown wild encounters (only available after solving puzzles)

---

name: Puzzle Chambers
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Puzzle Chambers

Each chamber has a sliding tile puzzle depicting a prehistoric/legendary Pokemon. Solving a puzzle unlocks a set of Unown letters and drops you into the Inner Chamber.

### 1. Kabuto Chamber
**Location:** Northeast corner of ruins (accessible from Inner Chamber north entrance)
**Puzzle:** Rearrange tiles to form Kabuto
**Description:** "A Pokemon that hid on the sea floor. Eyes on its back scanned the area."
**Wall Hint:** ESCAPE (written in Unown text)
**Secret:** Use an Escape Rope while in the chamber to open the hidden wall
**Unlocks:** Unown A through K (ENGINE_UNLOCKED_UNOWNS_A_TO_K)
**Hidden Items (Item Room):** Berry, PsnCureBerry, Heal Powder, EnergyPowder

### 2. Omanyte Chamber
**Location:** Southwest corner (accessible from Inner Chamber west entrance)
**Puzzle:** Rearrange tiles to form Omanyte
**Wall Hint:** WATER (written in Unown text)
**Secret:** Have a Water Stone in your bag while in the chamber
**Unlocks:** Unown L through R (ENGINE_UNLOCKED_UNOWNS_L_TO_R)
**Hidden Items (Item Room):** MysteryBerry, Mystic Water, Stardust, Star Piece

### 3. Aerodactyl Chamber
**Location:** Southeast corner (accessible from Inner Chamber east entrance)
**Puzzle:** Rearrange tiles to form Aerodactyl
**Wall Hint:** LIGHT (written in Unown text)
**Secret:** Use Flash while in the chamber
**Unlocks:** Unown S through W (ENGINE_UNLOCKED_UNOWNS_S_TO_W)
**Hidden Items (Item Room):** Gold Berry, Moon Stone, Heal Powder, Energy Root

### 4. Ho-Oh Chamber
**Location:** Northwest corner (accessible from Inner Chamber south entrance)
**Puzzle:** Rearrange tiles to form Ho-Oh
**Wall Hint:** HO OH (written in Unown text)
**Secret:** Have Ho-Oh in your party while in the chamber
**Unlocks:** Unown X through Z (ENGINE_UNLOCKED_UNOWNS_X_TO_Z)
**Hidden Items (Item Room):** Gold Berry, MysteryBerry, Revival Herb, Charcoal

---

name: Unlockable Unown Letters
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Unlockable Unown Letters

Unown forms are unlocked in 4 sets, one per puzzle:

| Puzzle Solved | Unown Letters Unlocked |
|--------------|----------------------|
| Kabuto | A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K (11 letters) |
| Omanyte | L, M, N, O, P, Q, R (7 letters) |
| Aerodactyl | S, T, U, V, W (5 letters) |
| Ho-Oh | X, Y, Z (3 letters) |

**Total:** 26 letters (A-Z)

After solving a puzzle, wild Unown matching those letters appear in the Inner Chamber (main hall with ruins). Walk around in the main chamber to encounter them.

---

name: Unown Pokedex Words
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Unown Pokedex Words

Each Unown letter has an associated word, visible in the Unown Pokedex mode (accessed from the Pokedex after catching at least 3 different Unown forms):

| Letter | Word |
|--------|------|
| A | ANGRY |
| B | BEAR |
| C | CHASE |
| D | DIRECT |
| E | ENGAGE |
| F | FIND |
| G | GIVE |
| H | HELP |
| I | INCREASE |

| K | KEEP |
| L | LAUGH |
| M | MAKE |
| N | NUZZLE |
| O | OBSERVE |
| P | PERFORM |
| Q | QUICKEN |
| R | REASSURE |
| S | SEARCH |
| T | TELL |
| U | UNDO |
| V | VANISH |
| W | WANT |
| X | XXXXX |
| Y | YIELD |
| Z | ZOOM |

**Note:** X's word is "XXXXX" — a placeholder since there's no good X word that matches the theme of verbs/actions.

---

name: Unown Stats and Mechanics
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Unown Stats and Mechanics

**Type:** Psychic
**Base Stats:** 48 HP / 72 Atk / 48 Def / 48 Spd / 72 SpAtk / 48 SpDef (BST: 336)
**Only Move:** Hidden Power (type and power vary by DVs)
**Catch Rate:** 225 (fairly easy)
**Wild Levels:** 5 (inner chamber encounters)

**Determining Unown's Letter:**
The Unown letter is determined by the Pokemon's DVs (IVs in Gen 2):
- Take the middle 2 bits of each DV: Atk, Def, Speed, Special
- Combine into an 8-bit value
- Divide by 10 to get the letter (0=A, 1=B, ..., 25=Z)
- This means different Unown letters have different Hidden Power types/powers

---

name: Inner Chamber
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Inner Chamber

The main hall of the ruins. Contains statues of ancient Pokemon and serves as the hub connecting all 4 puzzle chambers.

**Warps to:**
- Ho-Oh Chamber (southwest)
- Kabuto Chamber (northeast)
- Omanyte Chamber (southwest lower)
- Aerodactyl Chamber (southeast lower)
- Ruins of Alph Outside (south exit)

After solving any puzzle, you fall through the floor into the Inner Chamber and a "strange presence" message appears. Wild Unown begin appearing.

---

name: Research Center
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Research Center

**Location:** South entrance of Ruins of Alph
**NPCs:**
- Scientists studying the ruins
- After catching 3+ Unown forms, a scientist gives you the Unown Pokedex upgrade
- After catching all 26 forms, the lead scientist congratulates you and gives you a print of the Unown alphabet

---

name: Hidden Messages and Lore
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Hidden Messages and Lore

The wall patterns in each chamber spell out messages in Unown text:

- **Kabuto Chamber:** "ESCAPE" — hints at using an Escape Rope to open the secret wall
- **Omanyte Chamber:** "WATER" — hints at needing a Water Stone
- **Aerodactyl Chamber:** "LIGHT" — hints at using Flash (HM05)
- **Ho-Oh Chamber:** "HO OH" — hints at bringing Ho-Oh in your party

The Ruins of Alph represent an ancient civilization that worshipped Unown as symbols of language and communication. The puzzle chambers each depict a Pokemon believed to be revered by the ancients — Kabuto, Omanyte, and Aerodactyl (fossil Pokemon) and Ho-Oh (legendary bird).

---

name: Item Summary
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Item Summary

All items obtainable in the Ruins of Alph:

**Kabuto Item Room:** Berry, PsnCureBerry, Heal Powder, EnergyPowder
**Ho-Oh Item Room:** Gold Berry, MysteryBerry, Revival Herb, Charcoal
**Omanyte Item Room:** MysteryBerry, Mystic Water, Stardust, Star Piece
**Aerodactyl Item Room:** Gold Berry, Moon Stone, Heal Powder, Energy Root

**Total unique items:** Berry, PsnCureBerry, Heal Powder (x2), EnergyPowder, Gold Berry (x2), MysteryBerry (x2), Revival Herb, Charcoal, Mystic Water, Stardust, Star Piece, Moon Stone, Energy Root

---

name: Walkthrough Tips
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Walkthrough Tips

1. Visit the Kabuto Chamber first (accessible earliest, northeast entrance from Route 32 or Violet City)
2. Solve the sliding puzzle to unlock Unown A-K
3. Catch 3 different Unown to get the Pokedex upgrade from the Research Center
4. Return later with Escape Rope, Water Stone, Flash, and Ho-Oh to unlock the hidden walls
5. Each hidden wall leads to an Item Room, then to a Word Room
6. Complete all 4 puzzles to unlock all 26 Unown forms
7. The Unown Pokedex is a collector's side quest — no major reward beyond completion

name: Gym 1: Falkner (Flying) — Violet City
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Gym 1: Falkner (Flying) — Violet City

**Falkner's Team:**
| Pokemon | Level | Moves | HP (approx) |
|---------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Pidgey | 7 | Tackle, Mud-Slap, Gust | ~24 |
| Pidgeotto | 9 | Tackle, Mud-Slap, Gust | ~34 |

**Gym Trainers:** Bird Keeper Abe (Spearow Lv9), Bird Keeper Rod (Pidgey Lv7 x2)

**Weaknesses:** Electric, Rock, Ice
**Resistances:** Grass, Bug, Fighting (immune to Ground)

**Strategy:**
- Easiest gym. Most starters at Lv10+ can power through.
- Geodude from Dark Cave (Route 31/46) walls everything with Rock-type resistance.
- Trade Bellsprout for Onix in Violet City for an easy win.
- Mud-Slap lowers accuracy — heal status if it stacks.
- If using Chikorita, overleveling to Lv10+ makes it manageable despite the type disadvantage.

**Recommended Counter Pokemon:** Geodude (Lv6+), Onix (trade), Mareep (if backtracked to Route 32)

**Difficulty: 1/10**

---

name: Gym 2: Bugsy (Bug) — Azalea Town
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Gym 2: Bugsy (Bug) — Azalea Town

**Bugsy's Team:**
| Pokemon | Level | Moves | HP (approx) |
|---------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Metapod | 14 | Tackle, String Shot, Harden | ~42 |
| Kakuna | 14 | Poison Sting, String Shot, Harden | ~40 |
| Scyther | 16 | Fury Cutter, Quick Attack, Leer | ~54 |

**Gym Trainers:** Bug Catchers Al, Benny, Josh

**Weaknesses:** Fire, Flying, Rock
**Key Threat:** Scyther's Fury Cutter doubles damage each consecutive hit (10→20→40→80→160)

**Strategy:**
- Metapod and Kakuna are harmless. Save your strong Pokemon for Scyther.
- **Critical**: Do NOT let Fury Cutter hit consecutively. Switch Pokemon to reset the counter.
- Cyndaquil/Quilava destroys this gym with Ember.
- Pidgeotto (Flying) is strong but watch out for Fury Cutter stacking.
- Geodude resists everything Scyther has.

**Recommended Counter Pokemon:** Quilava (Lv14+), Geodude/Onix, Pidgeotto, any Fire type

**Difficulty: 3/10** (Scyther can surprise you if unprepared)

---

name: Gym 3: Whitney (Normal) — Goldenrod City
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Gym 3: Whitney (Normal) — Goldenrod City

**Whitney's Team:**
| Pokemon | Level | Moves | HP (approx) |
|---------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Clefairy | 18 | Encore, DoubleSlap, Mimic, Metronome | ~63 |
| Miltank | 20 | Stomp, Attract, Milk Drink, Rollout | ~80 |

**Gym Trainers:** Beauty Victoria, Beauty Samantha, Lass Carrie, Lass Bridget

**Weaknesses:** Fighting (Normal has no resistances except Ghost immunity)
**Key Threats:** Miltank's Rollout (doubles each turn, can OHKO after 3-4 hits), Attract (male Pokemon can't attack 50% of time), Milk Drink (heals 50% HP)

**Strategy:**
- **The hardest early gym.** Many players lose here.
- Clefairy's Encore can lock you into a bad move; Metronome is unpredictable.
- **Miltank**: Attract only affects male Pokemon. Use a **female** Pokemon if possible.
- **Rollout strategy**: The move doubles each turn and has 90% accuracy. Interrupt it by making Miltank faint, miss, or switch. If it connects 5 times, it can KO anything.
- Trade Drowzee for **Machop** in Goldenrod Dept Store — Machop is Fighting-type and female, countering both Attract and Normal typing.
- Geodude/Graveler resists Stomp/Rollout and can hit back with Rock Throw.
- Status moves (Paralysis, Sleep) can stop the Rollout chain.
- Milk Drink means you need burst damage, not chip damage.

**Recommended Counter Pokemon:** Machop (trade, female, Fighting), Geodude/Graveler, Heracross (headbutt), any female Pokemon with strong attacks

**Difficulty: 7/10** (hardest gym in early game)

**Note:** Whitney cries after losing. You must talk to the Lass in the gym to receive the badge.

---

name: Gym 4: Morty (Ghost) — Ecruteak City
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Gym 4: Morty (Ghost) — Ecruteak City

**Morty's Team:**
| Pokemon | Level | Moves | HP (approx) |
|---------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Gastly | 21 | Lick, Spite, Mean Look, Curse | ~45 |
| Haunter | 21 | Hypnosis, Mimic, Curse, Night Shade | ~55 |
| Haunter | 23 | Hypnosis, Mimic, Curse, Night Shade | ~58 |
| Gengar | 25 | Hypnosis, Shadow Ball, Mean Look, Dream Eater | ~72 |

**Weaknesses:** Ghost, Dark (but Ghost is Physical in Gen 2, Dark is Special)
**Resistances:** Poison, Bug (immune to Normal, Fighting)

**Strategy:**
- Normal and Fighting moves are completely useless (immunity).
- **Hypnosis** is the biggest threat — bring Awakenings or use Chesto Berry/Mint Berry.
- Gengar's combo: Hypnosis → Dream Eater (heals Gengar). Mean Look traps you.
- Gen 2 quirk: Ghost moves (Shadow Ball, Lick) use the Attack stat. Dark moves (Bite, Crunch) use Special Attack.
- **Best counters**: Pokemon with Bite (Raticate, Croconaw, Gyarados) or Crunch, or high-level Pokemon that can tank hits.
- Umbreon (if you evolved Eevee at night) has Dark typing and resists Ghost moves.
- Kadabra with high SpA can use non-Ghost/non-Normal moves effectively.

**Recommended Counter Pokemon:** Umbreon, anything with Bite/Crunch, Kadabra (Psych Up or raw power), Raticate (Hyper Fang + Bite)

**Difficulty: 5/10** (annoying Hypnosis spam, but manageable)

---

name: Gym 5: Chuck (Fighting) — Cianwood City
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Gym 5: Chuck (Fighting) — Cianwood City

**Chuck's Team:**
| Pokemon | Level | Moves | HP (approx) |
|---------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Primeape | 27 | Leer, Rage, Karate Chop, Fury Swipes | ~82 |
| Poliwrath | 30 | Hypnosis, Mind Reader, Surf, DynamicPunch | ~105 |

**Weaknesses:** Flying, Psychic (Poliwrath also weak to Electric, Grass)
**Key Threat:** Poliwrath's Hypnosis → Mind Reader → DynamicPunch (never misses after Mind Reader, causes Confusion)

**Strategy:**
- Primeape is straightforward — any Flying or Psychic type handles it.
- **Poliwrath** is the real fight. Water/Fighting with Hypnosis makes it tricky.
- Hypnosis → Mind Reader → guaranteed DynamicPunch. This confuses + deals heavy damage.
- Bring Awakenings. Status heals are critical.
- Electric types (Ampharos, Magneton) hit Poliwrath super-effectively.
- Grass types (Meganium, Victreebel) are 4x effective but watch Surf.
- Kadabra/Alakazam with Psychic one-shots most of Chuck's team.

**Recommended Counter Pokemon:** Kadabra, Ampharos, Pidgeotto/Pidgeot, any Psychic or Electric

**Difficulty: 4/10**

---

name: Gym 6: Jasmine (Steel) — Olivine City
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Gym 6: Jasmine (Steel) — Olivine City

**Jasmine's Team:**
| Pokemon | Level | Moves | HP (approx) |
|---------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Magnemite | 30 | Thunderbolt, Supersonic, SonicBoom, Thunder Wave | ~68 |
| Magnemite | 30 | Thunderbolt, Supersonic, SonicBoom, Thunder Wave | ~68 |
| Steelix | 35 | Iron Tail, Screech, Sunny Day, Rock Throw | ~115 |

**Weaknesses:** Fire, Fighting, Ground (Magnemite: Ground 4x; Steelix: Fire, Water, Fighting, Ground)
**Key Threat:** Steelix has massive Defense (200 base); Screech halves your Defense; Iron Tail hits hard

**Strategy:**
- Magnemites are weak — Earthquake or any Ground move is 4x effective. Even Dig works.
- Steelix: DO NOT use physical attacks unless you have very high Attack. Its Defense is enormous.
- **Special attacks are key**: Surf, Flamethrower, special-side Fire/Water moves.
- Typhlosion with Flamethrower/Fire Blast demolishes this gym.
- Feraligatr with Surf is effective.
- Steelix sets up Sunny Day (weakens Water, boosts Fire?) — this actually helps Fire-type challengers.
- Quagsire (Water/Ground) is immune to Thunderbolt and hits everything SE.

**Recommended Counter Pokemon:** Typhlosion, Feraligatr/Croconaw, Quagsire, anything with Earthquake, Machoke

**Difficulty: 4/10** (predictable, but Steelix is a wall if unprepared)

---

name: Gym 7: Pryce (Ice) — Mahogany Town
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Gym 7: Pryce (Ice) — Mahogany Town

**Pryce's Team:**
| Pokemon | Level | Moves | HP (approx) |
|---------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Seel | 27 | Headbutt, Icy Wind, Rest, Snore | ~85 |
| Dewgong | 29 | Aurora Beam, Ice Beam, Rest, Sleep Talk | ~105 |
| Piloswine | 31 | Blizzard, Icy Wind, Fury Attack, Mist | ~110 |

**Weaknesses:** Fire, Fighting, Rock, Steel (Piloswine: Water, Grass, Fighting, Steel — 4x to each except Fire)
**Key Threat:** Dewgong's Rest + Sleep Talk combo makes it annoyingly tanky; Piloswine's Blizzard hits hard

**Strategy:**
- Seel is easy. Just hit it hard before it Rests.
- Dewgong: Rest + Sleep Talk means it heals and attacks while sleeping. Bring strong attackers to overwhelm it.
- Piloswine: Ice/Ground typing. 4x weak to Water and Grass. Surf or Razor Leaf one-shots.
- Typhlosion resists Ice but watch Piloswine's Ground typing (neutral to Fire).
- Machoke/Machamp with Fighting moves cleans up.
- Red Gyarados works well here.

**Recommended Counter Pokemon:** Feraligatr (Surf), Ampharos, Machoke, Red Gyarados, Typhlosion

**Difficulty: 3/10**

---

name: Gym 8: Clair (Dragon) — Blackthorn City
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Gym 8: Clair (Dragon) — Blackthorn City

**Clair's Team:**
| Pokemon | Level | Moves | HP (approx) |
|---------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Dragonair | 37 | Thunder Wave, Surf, DragonBreath, Slam | ~120 |
| Dragonair | 37 | Thunder Wave, Thunderbolt, DragonBreath, Slam | ~120 |
| Dragonair | 37 | Thunder Wave, Ice Beam, DragonBreath, Slam | ~120 |
| Kingdra | 40 | Surf, Hyper Beam, DragonBreath, SmokeScreen | ~140 |

**Weaknesses:** Dragon, Ice (Dragonair); Dragon only (Kingdra — Water/Dragon has almost no weaknesses)
**Key Threat:** Kingdra. Water/Dragon typing means only Dragon-type moves are super-effective. SmokeScreen lowers accuracy.

**Strategy:**
- **Hardest gym in Johto.** Clair's team is high-level and well-constructed.
- Each Dragonair has different coverage (Surf, Thunderbolt, Ice Beam) — type counters are unreliable.
- Thunder Wave from all 3 Dragonair means paralysis is constant. Bring Parlyz Heals.
- **Kingdra** is the real boss. Weak ONLY to Dragon. In Gen 2, the only Dragon moves available are DragonBreath (60 power) and Outrage (120 but confusion).
- Strategy: Overpower Kingdra with high-level neutral attacks. STAB Hyper Beam from Kingdra hurts.
- **Lapras with Ice Beam** handles the 3 Dragonair easily; use brute force on Kingdra.
- Piloswine/Jynx from Ice Path provide Ice coverage.
- SmokeScreen + Hyper Beam on Kingdra — use X Accuracy items or moves that can't miss.

**Recommended Counter Pokemon:** Lapras (Ice Beam for Dragonair, tank Kingdra), Piloswine, Jynx, any Lv40+ Pokemon with strong neutral moves

**Difficulty: 8/10**

**Note:** After winning, Clair refuses the badge. Must complete Dragon's Den quiz to receive Rising Badge.

---

## Elite Four #1: Will (Psychic)

name: Will's Team
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Will's Team

| Pokemon | Level | Type | Moves | Strategy Notes |
|---------|-------|------|-------|----------------|
| Xatu | 40 | Psychic/Flying | Confuse Ray, Future Sight, Quick Attack, Psychic | Confuse Ray is annoying |
| Jynx | 41 | Ice/Psychic | DoubleSlap, Ice Punch, Lovely Kiss, Psychic | Lovely Kiss = sleep |
| Exeggutor | 41 | Grass/Psychic | Reflect, Leech Seed, Psychic, Egg Bomb | Leech Seed + Reflect stalls |
| Slowbro | 41 | Water/Psychic | Curse, Amnesia, Body Slam, Psychic | Curse + Amnesia = unkillable if let set up |
| Xatu | 42 | Psychic/Flying | Confuse Ray, Future Sight, Quick Attack, Psychic | Slightly higher level |

name: Weaknesses by Pokemon
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Weaknesses by Pokemon

- Xatu: Electric, Ice, Rock, Ghost, Dark (5 weaknesses)
- Jynx: Fire, Rock, Bug, Ghost, Dark, Steel (6 weaknesses)
- Exeggutor: Fire, Ice (4x), Flying, Poison, Bug (4x), Ghost, Dark
- Slowbro: Electric, Grass, Bug, Ghost, Dark

name: Strategy
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Strategy

- **Dark-type moves** hit everything super-effectively. Umbreon with Pursuit/Faint Attack works.
- **Bug-type moves** (Megahorn from Heracross) are devastating — Exeggutor takes 4x.
- Jynx: Hit fast before Lovely Kiss (sleep). Fire or Rock attacks OHKO.
- Slowbro: DO NOT let it use Curse + Amnesia. Hit hard with Electric/Grass immediately.
- Exeggutor: Fire or Ice Beam. Any Ice move does 4x damage.

name: Recommended Lead: Umbreon (Dark), Heracross (Bug/Fighting), or Typhlosion (Fire)
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Recommended Lead: Umbreon (Dark), Heracross (Bug/Fighting), or Typhlosion (Fire)

**Difficulty: 3/10**

---

## Elite Four #2: Koga (Poison)

name: Koga's Team
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Koga's Team

| Pokemon | Level | Type | Moves | Strategy Notes |
|---------|-------|------|-------|----------------|
| Ariados | 40 | Bug/Poison | Double Team, Giga Drain, Baton Pass, Spider Web | Traps + passes evasion |
| Venomoth | 41 | Bug/Poison | Gust, Supersonic, Psychic, Toxic | Toxic + Supersonic combo |
| Forretress | 43 | Bug/Steel | Protect, Swift, Explosion, Spikes | Spikes chip + Explosion |
| Muk | 42 | Poison | Minimize, Toxic, Sludge Bomb, Acid Armor | Evasion stacking nightmare |
| Crobat | 44 | Poison/Flying | Double Team, Wing Attack, Toxic, Confuse Ray | Fast, evasive, annoying |

name: Weaknesses by Pokemon
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Weaknesses by Pokemon

- Ariados: Fire, Flying, Rock, Psychic
- Venomoth: Fire, Flying, Rock, Psychic
- Forretress: Fire (4x only weakness!)
- Muk: Psychic, Ground
- Crobat: Electric, Ice, Psychic, Rock

name: Strategy
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Strategy

- **Koga is annoying, not hard.** His team stalls with Toxic, Double Team, and confusion.
- Bring **Full Heals / Full Restores** — status is Koga's main weapon.
- **Forretress**: Only weak to Fire (4x). Flamethrower/Fire Blast one-shots. Beware Explosion (huge damage, KOs Forretress too).
- **Muk**: Minimize makes it nearly unhittable. Use Swift (never misses), or high-accuracy moves. Earthquake/Psychic before it sets up.
- **Ariados** can trap with Spider Web and Baton Pass Double Team boosts. KO it fast.
- **Psychic-type moves** hit 4 of 5 Pokemon super-effectively. Alakazam or Espeon sweeps.
- **Ground moves** (Earthquake) hit Muk, and Fire handles the rest.

name: Recommended Lead: Alakazam/Espeon (Psychic) or Typhlosion (Fire)
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Recommended Lead: Alakazam/Espeon (Psychic) or Typhlosion (Fire)

**Difficulty: 4/10** (annoying evasion/status, but not powerful)

## Elite Four #3: Bruno (Fighting)

name: Bruno's Team
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Bruno's Team

| Pokemon | Level | Type | Moves | Strategy Notes |
|---------|-------|------|-------|----------------|
| Hitmontop | 42 | Fighting | Quick Attack, Pursuit, Counter, Detect | Counter reflects double damage |
| Hitmonlee | 42 | Fighting | Hi Jump Kick, Foresight, Swagger, Double Kick | Hi Jump Kick crashes on miss |
| Hitmonchan | 42 | Fighting | ThunderPunch, Ice Punch, Fire Punch, Mach Punch | Elemental punches = surprise coverage |
| Onix | 43 | Rock/Ground | Bind, Rock Slide, Earthquake, Sandstorm | Not Fighting; different weaknesses |
| Machamp | 46 | Fighting | Rock Slide, Foresight, Vital Throw, Cross Chop | Highest level, heavy hitter |

name: Weaknesses by Pokemon
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Weaknesses by Pokemon

- Hitmontop/Hitmonlee/Hitmonchan/Machamp: Flying, Psychic
- Onix: Water (4x), Grass (4x), Ice, Fighting, Ground, Steel

name: Strategy
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Strategy

- **Psychic or Flying** moves sweep most of Bruno's team.
- **Hitmontop**: Beware Counter — if you use physical moves, it reflects double damage. Use special attacks.
- **Hitmonlee**: Hi Jump Kick has crash damage on miss. It can hurt itself.
- **Hitmonchan**: The elemental punches (Thunder/Ice/Fire Punch) are all Special in Gen 2. They can surprise type-resistant Pokemon. Flying-types beware of ThunderPunch; Ice-types beware of Fire Punch.
- **Onix**: Surf one-shots (4x effective). Not a Fighting type — different counter needed.
- **Machamp** at Lv46: Highest level. Cross Chop is devastating. Foresight removes Ghost immunity. Psychic one-shots.

name: Recommended Lead: Alakazam/Espeon (Psychic sweeps 4/5), Pidgeot/Crobat (Flying for Fighting coverage)
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Recommended Lead: Alakazam/Espeon (Psychic sweeps 4/5), Pidgeot/Crobat (Flying for Fighting coverage)

**Difficulty: 3/10** (straightforward type matchups)

## Elite Four #4: Karen (Dark)

name: Karen's Team
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Karen's Team

| Pokemon | Level | Type | Moves | Strategy Notes |
|---------|-------|------|-------|----------------|
| Umbreon | 42 | Dark | Confuse Ray, Sand-Attack, Faint Attack, Mean Look | Stall + trap combo |
| Vileplume | 42 | Grass/Poison | Petal Dance, Stun Spore, Acid, Moonlight | Stun Spore → Petal Dance |
| Gengar | 45 | Ghost/Poison | Lick, Spite, Curse, Destiny Bond | Destiny Bond KOs your Pokemon |
| Murkrow | 44 | Dark/Flying | Whirlwind, Pursuit, Faint Attack, Quick Attack | Whirlwind disrupts setup |
| Houndoom | 47 | Dark/Fire | Flamethrower, Crunch, Pursuit, Roar | Highest level; strong attacker |

name: Weaknesses by Pokemon
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Weaknesses by Pokemon

- Umbreon: Fighting, Bug
- Vileplume: Fire, Ice, Flying, Psychic
- Gengar: Ghost, Dark, Psychic, Ground (but Levitate... no, Gen 2 no abilities)
- Murkrow: Electric, Ice, Rock
- Houndoom: Fighting, Ground, Rock, Water

name: Strategy
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Strategy

- Karen has the most diverse team. No single type counters everything.
- **Umbreon**: Tanky staller. Confuse Ray + Sand-Attack + Mean Look traps and annoys. Use Fighting moves (Machamp) or just powerful neutral hits. It will take a while to go down.
- **Vileplume**: Stun Spore is dangerous. Fire Blast or Psychic one-shots. Don't let it get Stun Spore off.
- **Gengar**: **BEWARE DESTINY BOND.** If you KO Gengar while Destiny Bond is active, your Pokemon faints too. Use weaker attacks to bait it, or attack the turn after Destiny Bond (it only lasts one turn). Curse also drains HP per turn.
- **Murkrow**: Weakest member. Whirlwind can force switches. Electric/Ice moves handle it.
- **Houndoom** at Lv47: Karen's ace. Flamethrower hits hard; Crunch is STAB; Pursuit punishes switching. Water or Fighting moves are best. Earthquake works if you have it.

name: Recommended Lead: Machamp (Fighting for Umbreon/Houndoom), Feraligatr (Water for Houndoom)
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Recommended Lead: Machamp (Fighting for Umbreon/Houndoom), Feraligatr (Water for Houndoom)

**Difficulty: 5/10** (Destiny Bond + Umbreon stalling can be tricky)

## Champion: Lance (Dragon)

name: Lance's Team
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Lance's Team

| Pokemon | Level | Type | Moves | Strategy Notes |
|---------|-------|------|-------|----------------|
| Gyarados | 44 | Water/Flying | Flail, Rain Dance, Surf, Hyper Beam | Sets up Rain Dance |
| Dragonite | 47 | Dragon/Flying | Thunder Wave, Twister, Thunder, Hyper Beam | Uses Thunder in Rain |
| Dragonite | 47 | Dragon/Flying | Thunder Wave, Twister, Thunder, Hyper Beam | Duplicate of above |
| Aerodactyl | 46 | Rock/Flying | Wing Attack, AncientPower, Rock Slide, Hyper Beam | Fast physical attacker |
| Dragonite | 50 | Dragon/Flying | Fire Blast, Safeguard, Outrage, Hyper Beam | Ace Dragonite; highest level |
| Charizard | 46 | Fire/Flying | Flamethrower, Wing Attack, Slash, Hyper Beam | Fire coverage |

name: Weaknesses by Pokemon
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Weaknesses by Pokemon

- Gyarados: Electric (4x), Rock
- Dragonite x3: Ice (4x), Dragon, Rock, Electric
- Aerodactyl: Water, Electric, Ice, Rock, Steel
- Charizard: Water, Electric, Rock (4x)

name: Strategy
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Strategy

- **Ice and Electric are the keys to this fight.**
- **Gyarados**: Thunder or Thunderbolt one-shots (4x weakness). It uses Rain Dance which boosts Thunder accuracy to 100% — this helps your Electric moves too!
- **Dragonite x3**: ICE BEAM. All three take 4x damage from Ice. One Ice Beam from a decent SpA Pokemon (Lapras, Jynx, Piloswine) OHKOs each one. They use Thunder Wave for paralysis — bring Parlyz Heals.
- **Aerodactyl**: Fast (Lv46, high Speed). Rock Slide can flinch. Ice Beam or Surf handles it. Electric works too.
- **Dragonite Lv50 (ace)**: Has Fire Blast and Outrage instead of Thunder. Outrage is devastating but causes self-confusion after 2-3 turns. Safeguard blocks status. Ice Beam still one-shots.
- **Charizard**: Surf, Thunderbolt, or Rock Slide. Rock is 4x effective. Stealth Rock would help... but doesn't exist in Gen 2.
- **Hyper Beam** on every Pokemon: After using it, the Pokemon recharges for one turn. This is a free turn to heal or set up.

name: The Ice Beam Strategy
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] The Ice Beam Strategy

If you have ONE Pokemon with Ice Beam and decent Special Attack (Lapras ideal):
1. Use Ice Beam on Dragonite #1 → OHKO
2. Use Ice Beam on Dragonite #2 → OHKO
3. Use Ice Beam on Dragonite #3 → OHKO
4. Thunderbolt for Gyarados/Charizard
5. Surf for Aerodactyl

**Lapras** (Union Cave B2F Friday) with Ice Beam + Thunderbolt + Surf + Psychic can theoretically solo Lance.

name: Recommended Team for Lance:
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Recommended Team for Lance:

1. Electric type with Thunderbolt (Ampharos, Raikou, Magneton)
2. Ice type with Ice Beam (Lapras, Piloswine, Jynx)
3. Water type for backup (Feraligatr, Gyarados)
4. Any strong backup for emergencies

**Difficulty: 6/10** (3 Dragonite is scary on paper, but Ice Beam trivializes it)

---

name: Pokemon Availability by Gym
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Pokemon Availability by Gym

### Before Gym 1 (Falkner, Flying — Violet City)
**Available Pokemon** (catchable before challenging Falkner):
- **Starter**: Chikorita / Cyndaquil / Totodile (Lv5)
- **Route 29**: Pidgey, Sentret, Rattata, Hoppip, Hoothoot (night)
- **Route 30**: Pidgey, Caterpie, Weedle, Ledyba (morn), Spinarak (night), Metapod
- **Route 31**: Pidgey, Bellsprout, Caterpie/Weedle, Poliwag (night)
- **Sprout Tower**: Gastly (night), Rattata
- **Dark Cave**: Geodude, Zubat (needs Flash — available from Sprout Tower)
- **Trade**: Bellsprout for Onix ("Rocky") in Violet City

**Best choices for Falkner**: Geodude (Rock resists Flying), Onix (trade), Mareep (Route 32, but past gym). Cyndaquil or Totodile are neutral matchups. Chikorita is weak here.

### Before Gym 2 (Bugsy, Bug — Azalea Town)
**New additions** (Route 32, Union Cave, Route 33):
- **Route 32**: Ekans, Bellsprout, Mareep, Wooper (night), Hoppip
- **Union Cave**: Geodude, Onix, Sandshrew, Zubat
- **Route 33**: Rattata, Spearow, Hoppip, Zubat (night)

**Best choices for Bugsy**: Geodude (resists Bug), Pidgey/Pidgeotto (Flying beats Bug), Cyndaquil/Quilava (Fire beats Bug). Watch out for Scyther's Fury Cutter doubling each hit.

### Before Gym 3 (Whitney, Normal — Goldenrod City)
**New additions** (Ilex Forest, Route 34):
- **Ilex Forest**: Oddish, Paras, Caterpie, Metapod, Zubat (night)
- **Route 34**: Drowzee, Rattata, Abra, Ditto
- **Goldenrod trades/gifts**: Machop (trade Drowzee at Dept Store)
- **Game Corner**: Abra (200 coins), Dratini (2100 coins)

**Best choices for Whitney**: **Machop** (trade in Goldenrod — Fighting beats Normal; comes with Gold Berry), Geodude/Graveler (resists Rollout, immune to Stomp), Female Pokemon (immune to Attract). Whitney's Miltank is notorious — use physical walls, Fighting types, or status moves.

### Before Gym 4 (Morty, Ghost — Ecruteak City)
**New additions** (Routes 35-37, National Park):
- **Route 35**: Nidoran-F, Nidoran-M, Drowzee, Abra, Yanma (rare 1%), Ditto
- **National Park**: Caterpie, Scyther/Pinsir (Bug Contest), Sunkern
- **Route 36**: Growlithe, Stantler, Bellsprout, Pidgey
- **Route 37**: Growlithe, Stantler, Pidgey/Pidgeotto

**Best choices for Morty**: Normal-type moves are immune to Ghost, but Ghost is immune to Normal too. Use Dark-type moves (Bite, Crunch, Pursuit) or just raw power. Umbreon, Murkrow, or anything with Bite works. Note: in Gen 2, Ghost is Physical and Dark is Special.

### Before Gym 5 (Chuck, Fighting — Cianwood City)
**New additions** (Routes 38-41):
- **Route 38-39**: Raticate, Tauros, Miltank, Magnemite, Farfetch'd, Meowth (night)
- **Olivine area**: Tentacool/Tentacruel (Surf)
- **Route 40-41**: Tentacool, Mantine (rare)

**Best choices for Chuck**: Flying (Pidgeotto/Pidgeot), Psychic (Drowzee/Hypno, Kadabra), Grass types. Poliwrath knows Hypnosis + Mind Reader + DynamicPunch combo — very dangerous.

### Before Gym 6 (Jasmine, Steel — Olivine City)
**Same pool** as Gym 5 plus anything caught getting to/from Cianwood.

**Best choices for Jasmine**: Fire (Quilava/Typhlosion, Growlithe), Fighting (Machop/Machoke), Ground (Geodude/Graveler/Golem, Sandshrew). Steelix has high Defense — special attacks work better.

### Before Gym 7 (Pryce, Ice — Mahogany Town)
**New additions** (Route 42-43):
- **Route 42**: Spearow, Zubat, Mareep, Mankey, Flaaffy
- **Mt. Mortar**: Machop, Machoke, Geodude, Graveler, Tyrogue (Karate King)
- **Route 43**: Pidgeotto, Flaaffy, Girafarig, Venonat (night)
- **Lake of Rage**: Red Gyarados (guaranteed shiny Lv30)

**Best choices for Pryce**: Fire (Typhlosion, Growlithe/Arcanine), Fighting (Machoke/Machamp), Electric (Ampharos, Magneton). Red Gyarados works well too.

### Before Gym 8 (Clair, Dragon — Blackthorn City)
**New additions** (Route 44, Ice Path):
- **Route 44**: Weepinbell, Tangela, Lickitung, Bellsprout, Poliwag (night)
- **Ice Path**: Swinub, Jynx, Delibird

**Best choices for Clair**: Ice moves are king — Ice Beam, Blizzard, Icy Wind. Piloswine (Ice Path), Jynx (Ice Path), Lapras (Union Cave Friday). Kingdra is Water/Dragon — only weak to Dragon. Use Dragon-type moves or brute force with strong neutral hits.

name: Type Coverage Analysis
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Type Coverage Analysis

### Best Type Combinations for Johto Playthrough

| Combo | Covers (SE) | Weak Gyms | Notes |

| Water/Electric/Ground | 9 types SE | Almost everything | Surf + Thunderbolt + Earthquake |
| Water/Fighting/Flying | 8 types SE | All gyms viable | Versatile coverage |
| Fire/Ice/Electric | 8 types SE | Covers Dragon | Great E4 prep |

### Key Types to Have on Your Team

1. **Water** (Surf is required HM; covers Fire, Ground, Rock) — Feraligatr, Lapras, Gyarados, Red Gyarados
2. **Electric** (covers Water, Flying) — Ampharos (Mareep from Route 32), Magneton, Raikou (post-Burned Tower)
3. **Fighting or Ground** (covers Normal, Steel, Rock) — Machoke, Primeape, Golem
4. **Ice** (covers Dragon, Flying, Grass, Ground) — Lapras, Piloswine, Ice Beam TM

name: Pokemon Tier List (In-Game Viability)
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Pokemon Tier List (In-Game Viability)

### S Tier (Carry the game)
- **Typhlosion** (Cyndaquil) — Excellent speed/SpA, few bad matchups
- **Feraligatr** (Totodile) — Physical beast, Surf slave, Ice Punch
- **Red Gyarados** — Free Lv30, great stats, Dragon Dance potential
- **Ampharos** — Available early (Route 32), reliable, tanks hits
- **Espeon** — Bill's Eevee + friendship, fast special sweeper

### A Tier (Very strong picks)
- **Arcanine** — Growlithe from Route 36, good stats all around
- **Lapras** — Union Cave Friday, Ice Beam + Surf, tanky
- **Kadabra** — Abra is hard to catch but Kadabra is very strong
- **Heracross** — Headbutt trees, Megahorn is devastating
- **Nidoking** — Moon Stone evolve, great mixed movepool
- **Crobat** — Friendship from Zubat, very fast, Fly user

### B Tier (Solid choices)
- **Meganium** (Chikorita) — Defensive, but struggles in many gyms
- **Machoke/Machamp** — Fighting coverage, trade to evolve
- **Golem** — Trade to evolve, great physical stats
- **Jynx** — Ice Path, strong for Clair/Lance
- **Piloswine** — Ice Path, Ground/Ice coverage
- **Magneton** — Route 38, Steel typing is defensive

### C Tier (Usable but outclassed)
- **Pidgeot** — Early bird, Fly user, but mediocre stats
- **Raticate** — Early, fast, but fragile
- **Quagsire** — Water/Ground, only weak to Grass
- **Stantler** — Route 36, decent but forgettable

name: Elite Four Team Preparation
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Elite Four Team Preparation

### Recommended Levels: 42-50

### Type Needs for E4:
- **Will (Psychic)**: Dark, Ghost, Bug moves
- **Koga (Poison)**: Psychic, Ground moves
- **Bruno (Fighting)**: Flying, Psychic moves
- **Karen (Dark)**: Fighting, Bug, Fairy (N/A Gen 2) moves
- **Lance (Dragon)**: Ice (critical), Electric, Rock moves

### Essential Moves for E4:
- **Ice Beam / Blizzard** — Destroys Lance's Dragonite x3
- **Thunderbolt / Thunder** — Covers Gyarados, Charizard support
- **Earthquake** — Universal coverage
- **Psychic** — Koga's Poison types
- **Surf** — General coverage, HM required

### Key Items to Stock:
- Full Restore x20+
- Revive x10+
- Max Potion x10+
- Full Heal x10+ (Koga loves status moves)

name: Overview
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Overview

Pokemon Crystal Any% Glitchless aims to beat the Elite Four and enter the Hall of Fame as fast as possible without using any glitches. The run takes approximately 3:15-4:00 depending on RNG and execution.

### Key Decisions
- **Starter**: Totodile (standard route) — Feraligatr has the best combination of physical stats, Surf STAB, and movepool for sweeping the game
- **Additional Pokemon**: Minimal — catch Kenya the Spearow (Route 35 NPC gift) for Fly, a Surf slave if needed
- **Shopping**: Highly optimized; buy only what's needed
- **Trainer Avoidance**: Skip all optional trainers

---

name: Game Setup
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Game Setup

### Options
- Text Speed: **Fast**
- Battle Scene: **Off** (saves animation time)
- Battle Style: **Set** (saves time vs Switch prompt, forces strategic play)
- Time: Set to **5:50 PM** (specific encounter table manipulation)

### Starter Selection
- Choose **Totodile**
- Check stats: Need at minimum 10 Special Attack DV (12+ preferred)
- Attack DV should be reasonable (10+)
- Female Totodile has slightly lower Attack range but works

---

name: Early Game Routing
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Early Game Routing

### New Bark Town → Elm's Lab
1. Go downstairs, get Pokegear from Mom
2. Go to Elm's Lab, select Totodile (right Poke Ball)
3. Check stats on status screen
4. Get Potion from aide
5. Exit east... wait, west to Route 29

### Route 29 → Cherrygrove → Mr. Pokemon
6. Walk to Cherrygrove (avoid tall grass by walking on edges)
7. Get Map Card from Guide Gent (mandatory)
8. Head north to Route 30
9. Skip all optional trainers
10. Mr. Pokemon's House → Mystery Egg + Pokedex

### Rival Battle 1
11. Silver has Chikorita Lv5 (type advantage for us)
12. Spam Scratch/Leer → kill with Scratch
13. Leer first turn is optimal to guarantee 3HKO

### Return to Elm
14. Run back to Elm's Lab
15. Name rival (single character, e.g., "A" — fastest)
16. Get 5 Poke Balls

---

name: Violet City Segment
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Violet City Segment

### Route 31 → Violet City
17. Walk through Route 30/31 to Violet City
18. Fight mandatory trainers only

### Sprout Tower (Skip in some routes)
- **Standard route**: Skip Sprout Tower entirely (Flash not needed for the run)
- Some routes fight Sage Li for experience

### Falkner
19. Totodile should be Lv9-10
20. Water Gun everything (SE vs Ground-type Mud-Slap coverage doesn't matter)
21. Pidgey: Water Gun 2-3x
22. Pidgeotto: Water Gun 2-3x (may need Potion)
23. Get Zephyr Badge

name: Azalea Town Segment
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Azalea Town Segment

### Route 32 → Union Cave → Route 33

### Bugsy
32. Croconaw by now (evolves at Lv18 ideally during this gym)

37. Gastly: Bite/Water Gun; Zubat: Water Gun; Bayleef: Rage/Bite

name: Victory Road + E4
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Victory Road + E4

### Route 27 → Victory Road
105. Fly to New Bark Town, Surf east
106. Tohjo Falls (use Waterfall)
107. Route 26 → Victory Road

### Victory Road
108. Solve puzzles quickly
109. Rival Battle 5: Ice Punch/Surf/ThunderPunch coverage

### Elite Four
110. **Will**: Surf + Ice Punch sweep
111. **Koga**: Surf everything (Forretress: use anything non-Bug)
112. **Bruno**: Surf Onix, Ice Punch Machamp, Surf rest
113. **Karen**: Surf Houndoom, Ice Punch Vileplume... Mixed strategy needed
114. **Lance**: Ice Punch all 3 Dragonite, ThunderPunch Gyarados/Charizard, Surf Aerodactyl

### Feraligatr's E4 Moveset
The ideal Feraligatr moveset for the E4:
- **Surf** (STAB, 95 power, 100 accuracy)
- **Ice Punch** (75 power, handles Dragon/Grass/Flying)
- **ThunderPunch** (75 power, handles Water/Flying)
- **Strength** or **Cut** (HM, filler for normal-resist situations)

---

name: Time-Saving Tips
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Time-Saving Tips

### Movement Optimization
- Hold B to run (bicycle replaces this after Goldenrod)
- Take shortest paths — hug walls to avoid grass
- Bicycle is faster than running (get it in Goldenrod)
- Fly to skip backtracking wherever possible

### Battle Optimization
- Never grind — fight only mandatory battles
- Use items during free turns (Hyper Beam recharge, opponent using non-damaging moves)
- Flee from wild encounters immediately
- One-shot everything with super-effective moves

### Shopping Optimization
- Buy in bulk at Goldenrod Dept Store (one trip)
- TM Ice Punch + ThunderPunch are the most important purchases
- Potions/Super Potions only — Full Restores are expensive and rarely needed

### RNG Management
- Critical hits can save or waste time
- Paralysis/Confusion from enemy moves can lose runs
- Whitney's Miltank is the biggest RNG factor in early game
- Avoid accuracy-lowering moves stacking (Mud-Slap, SmokeScreen)

---

name: Common Run-Killers
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Common Run-Killers

1. **Whitney's Miltank** — Rollout chains can sweep your team
2. **Rival in Victory Road** — If underleveled, he can end the run
3. **Lance's Dragonite** — If Ice Punch doesn't OHKO, Thunder Wave + Hyper Beam can kill
4. **Clair's Kingdra** — No super-effective coverage; war of attrition
5. **Random critical hits** — Enemy crits on key Pokemon can force resets
6. **Hypnosis/Sleep** — Morty, Chuck, Will's Jynx all use sleep moves

---

name: Category Variants
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Category Variants

### Any% (with Glitches)
- Uses Coin Case glitch for arbitrary code execution
- Can beat the game in ~5 minutes
- Not representative of normal gameplay

### Any% Glitchless (Manipless)
- No RNG manipulation
- Longer due to suboptimal DVs and encounter RNG
- Times around 3:30-4:00

### Any% Glitchless (Manipulated)
- Pre-set RNG seed for perfect DVs and encounters
- Catches Raikou early for sweeping
- Fastest legitimate category

### Catch 'Em All
- Must obtain all 251 Pokemon
- 20+ hour runs
- Requires extensive trading and version-exclusive planning

name: Overview
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Overview

Generation 2 (Gold/Silver/Crystal) competitive play, commonly abbreviated GSC, is defined by a few key characteristics:
- **Snorlax dominance** — the most centralizing Pokemon in any generation's OU tier
- **Spikes-based gameplay** — Spikes is the primary hazard and shapes team building
- **Slow, strategic pace** — fewer sweepers, more walls and pivots
- **No abilities** — abilities didn't exist until Gen 3
- **No held item diversity** — Leftovers on everything (items exist but Leftovers dominates)
- **Physical/Special split by type** — not per move (Ghost is Physical, Dark is Special)

---

name: Tier List
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Tier List

### Banned (Ubers)
| Pokemon | Type | Reason |
|---------|------|--------|
| Mewtwo | Psychic | Overwhelming SpA (154) + Speed (130) + movepool |
| Mew | Psychic | 100 in all stats + every TM/HM learnable |
| Lugia | Psychic/Flying | Insane bulk (106/130/154 defenses) + Aeroblast |
| Ho-Oh | Fire/Flying | Sacred Fire burn chance + massive stats |
| Celebi | Psychic/Grass | Heal Bell + Recover + Leech Seed + Baton Pass |

### S Tier (Mandatory)
| Pokemon | Type | Base Stats | Role |
|---------|------|-----------|------|
| **Snorlax** | Normal | 160/110/65/65/110/30 | The single best Pokemon. Curse sweeper, mixed attacker, special wall. ~95% usage rate. |

### A+ Tier (Elite)
| Pokemon | Type | Base Stats | Role |
|---------|------|-----------|------|
| **Raikou** | Electric | 90/85/75/115/100/115 | Premier special wall, best Spikes shuffler, Sleep Talk + Roar |
| **Zapdos** | Electric/Flying | 90/90/85/125/90/100 | RestTalk pivot, Thunder, defensive anchor |
| **Tyranitar** | Rock/Dark | 100/134/110/95/100/61 | Mixed attacker, Curse sweeper, Pursuit trapper |
| **Skarmory** | Steel/Flying | 65/80/140/40/70/70 | Premier physical wall, Whirlwind phazer, Spikes |
| **Suicune** | Water | 100/75/115/90/115/85 | Bulky water, Roar phazer, RestTalk tank |

name: Key Mechanics for Competitive Play
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Key Mechanics for Competitive Play

### Spikes
- Only Cloyster and Forretress learn Spikes
- Up to 3 layers: 1/8, 1/6, 1/4 of max HP on switch-in
- No Stealth Rock in Gen 2 (introduced Gen 4)
- Rapid Spin removes Spikes (Starmie, Forretress, Cloyster are key spinners)
- Spikes + phazing (Roar/Whirlwind) is the primary strategy for wearing teams down

### Sleep Mechanics
- Sleep Talk: Use a random move while asleep (key for RestTalk strategy)
- Only 1 Pokemon can be put to sleep per team (Sleep Clause)
- Sleep lasts 1-7 turns (random)
- Rest: Fully heals HP + status, puts user to sleep for 2 turns exactly

### Critical Hits
- Base rate: ~6.25% (1/16)
- Focus Energy + Scope Lens can increase rate significantly
- Critical hits in Gen 2 ignore negative stat changes on attacker AND positive stat changes on defender

### Leftovers
- Recovers 1/16 of max HP per turn
- Almost every competitive Pokemon holds Leftovers
- No item clause in standard play (multiple Leftovers allowed)

### Baton Pass
- Passes stat boosts and substitutes to the next Pokemon
- Key users: Espeon (Growth), Jolteon (Agility), Vaporeon (Acid Armor)
- Baton Pass chains are viable but fragile

name: Key Threats Analysis
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Key Threats Analysis

### Snorlax — "The King"
**Why it's broken:**
- 160 HP / 110 SpDef makes it the best special wall
- 110 Attack lets it hit hard physically
- Curse boosts Attack + Defense at the cost of Speed (already slow — doesn't matter)
- Belly Drum can maximize Attack in one turn
- Movepool: Double-Edge, Earthquake, Fire Blast, Self-Destruct, Body Slam, Rest, Sleep Talk
- Every team must have a Snorlax answer or lose

**Common Sets:**
1. **CurseLax**: Curse / Double-Edge / Earthquake / Rest or Fire Blast
2. **DrumLax**: Belly Drum / Double-Edge / Earthquake / Rest
3. **MixLax**: Double-Edge / Earthquake / Fire Blast / Self-Destruct or Rest
4. **RestTalkLax**: Rest / Sleep Talk / Double-Edge / Earthquake or Curse

**Counters:** Machamp (Cross Chop), Heracross (Megahorn), Skarmory (walls physical), Tyranitar (Pursuit on switch)

### Skarmory — "The Wall"
**Why it's elite:**
- 140 Defense walls almost all physical attackers
- Whirlwind forces switches (racks up Spikes damage)
- Steel/Flying typing gives many resistances + immunities
- Only weak to Fire and Electric

**Counters:** Raikou (Thunderbolt), Zapdos (Thunder), Gengar (can't be Whirlwinded as Ghost)

### Raikou — "The Pivot"
**Why it's elite:**
- 115 SpA + 115 Speed = fast and strong
- Roar forces switches (Spikes damage)
- RestTalk makes it hard to wear down
- Checks more than half the metagame

**Counters:** Snorlax (tanks Thunderbolt), Tyranitar (resists Electric), Steelix (Ground immunity to Electric... wait no, Steel/Ground takes neutral from Electric)

name: Banned Moves and Clauses
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Banned Moves and Clauses

### Standard Clauses (Smogon)
- **Sleep Clause**: Only 1 opponent Pokemon may be put to sleep at a time
- **Species Clause**: No duplicate Pokemon on a team
- **Evasion Clause**: Double Team and Minimize are banned
- **OHKO Clause**: Fissure, Horn Drill, Guillotine are banned
- **Freeze Clause**: Only 1 Pokemon may be frozen at a time (sometimes applied)

### Notable Banned/Restricted
- No Pokemon from the Ubers list (Mewtwo, Mew, Lugia, Ho-Oh, Celebi)
- Swagger + Paralysis combo ("SwagPlay") is controversial but not officially banned in GSC
- Baton Pass is fully legal in standard GSC OU

name: Meta-Game Evolution
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Meta-Game Evolution

### Early GSC Meta (late 1990s - early 2000s)
- Dominated by Snorlax + Zapdos
- Spikes was underrated initially
- Stall teams were less refined

### Modern GSC Meta (2010s - present)
- Spikes is recognized as the most important move
- Forretress + Cloyster valued highly for Spikes support
- Raikou rose to become the #2 Pokemon (best shuffler)
- Perish trap strategies became more refined
- Team building is highly optimized around Snorlax matchup
- ~20-25 truly viable OU Pokemon (small metagame by modern standards)

### Design Philosophy
GSC rewards:
- **Patience**: Games are long; chip damage from Spikes/Toxic adds up
- **Prediction**: Switching is critical; wrong switches eat Spikes + attacks
- **Team synergy**: Every member must handle specific threats
- **Risk management**: Explosion, Self-Destruct, and Belly Drum are high-risk/high-reward

name: #143 Snorlax [OU]
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] #143 Snorlax [OU]

**Role:** Special Wall / Tank / CurseLax  
**Stats:** 160/110/65/30/65/110 (HP/Atk/Def/Spd/SpAtk/SpDef)

### Recommended Sets

- **CurseLax: Curse / Rest / Sleep Talk / Body Slam or Double-Edge**
- **MixLax: Body Slam / Earthquake / Fire Blast or Flamethrower / Rest or Self-Destruct**
- **DrumLax: Belly Drum / Body Slam / Earthquake / Rest**

### Why It's Good
The king of GSC OU. Snorlax's enormous HP and Special Defense make it the premier special tank. CurseLax is the most dangerous late-game sweeper. It fits on virtually every team.

### Counters
Skarmory walls physical sets. Misdreavus blocks Body Slam. Machamp with Cross Chop. Tyranitar can trade blows. Roar/Whirlwind users to phaze CurseLax.

---

name: #248 Tyranitar [OU]
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] #248 Tyranitar [OU]

**Role:** Mixed Attacker / Pursuit Trapper  
**Stats:** 100/134/110/61/95/100 (HP/Atk/Def/Spd/SpAtk/SpDef)

### Recommended Sets

- **MixTar: Rock Slide / Fire Blast / DynamicPunch or Earthquake / Pursuit**
- **CurseTar: Curse / Rock Slide / Earthquake / Rest**

### Why It's Good
Massive 600 BST with excellent mixed attacking stats. Pursuit traps Gengar and Starmie. DynamicPunch always confuses. Sets Sandstorm with its mere presence (not literally - no Sand Stream yet).

### Counters
Machamp, Heracross, Marowak, Steelix. 4x weak to Fighting. Water-types like Suicune and Vaporeon.

---

name: #145 Zapdos [OU]
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] #145 Zapdos [OU]

**Role:** Special Attacker / RestTalker  
**Stats:** 90/90/85/100/125/90 (HP/Atk/Def/Spd/SpAtk/SpDef)

### Recommended Sets

- **RestTalk: Thunder / Hidden Power Ice / Rest / Sleep Talk**
- **Offensive: Thunderbolt / Hidden Power Ice or Water / Thunder Wave / Rest**

### Why It's Good
Best Electric-type in GSC. Excellent typing with only two weaknesses. Great bulk and power. Threatens Waters and Skarmory.

### Counters
Snorlax (with high SpDef), Raikou, Blissey, Ground-types like Rhydon/Golem (immune to Thunder).

name: #227 Skarmory [OU]
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] #227 Skarmory [OU]

**Role:** Physical Wall / Spiker / Phazer  
**Stats:** 65/80/140/70/40/70 (HP/Atk/Def/Spd/SpAtk/SpDef)

### Recommended Sets

- **Spiker: Spikes / Whirlwind / Drill Peck / Rest or Toxic**
- **CursKarm: Curse / Drill Peck / Rest / Sleep Talk**

### Why It's Good
The premier physical wall. Immune to Ground, resists Normal. Spikes + Whirlwind is incredibly effective for passive damage. Walls Snorlax, Marowak, and physical attackers.

### Counters
Zapdos, Raikou (4x weak to Electric). Fire-types like Charizard. Special attackers bypass its pitiful SpDef.

name: #121 Starmie [OU]
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] #121 Starmie [OU]

**Role:** Rapid Spinner / Special Attacker  
**Stats:** 60/75/85/115/100/85 (HP/Atk/Def/Spd/SpAtk/SpDef)

### Recommended Sets

- **Spinner: Rapid Spin / Surf / Psychic or Thunderbolt / Recover**
- **Offensive: Surf / Thunderbolt / Psychic / Recover**

### Why It's Good
The best Rapid Spinner in GSC. Blazing speed. Good coverage with Surf/Thunderbolt/Psychic. Recover for sustainability. Vital for removing Spikes.

### Counters
Pursuit Tyranitar traps it. Snorlax tanks hits. Blissey. Dark-types immune to Psychic.

name: #245 Suicune [OU]
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] #245 Suicune [OU]

**Role:** Bulky Water / CroSuicune  
**Stats:** 100/75/115/85/90/115 (HP/Atk/Def/Spd/SpAtk/SpDef)

### Recommended Sets

- **CroSuicune: Surf / Ice Beam / Rest / Sleep Talk**
- **Roar: Surf / Roar / Toxic / Rest**

### Why It's Good
Extreme physical and special bulk. RestTalk makes it very hard to kill. Roar variant is a great phazer that racks up Spikes damage.

### Counters
Zapdos, Raikou (Electric attacks). Vaporeon with Water Absorb. Snorlax can outlast. Grass-types.

name: #103 Exeggutor [OU]
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] #103 Exeggutor [OU]

**Role:** Sleep Lead / Special Attacker / Exploder  
**Stats:** 95/95/85/55/125/65 (HP/Atk/Def/Spd/SpAtk/SpDef)

### Recommended Sets

- **Standard: Sleep Powder / Psychic / Giga Drain or Hidden Power Fire / Explosion**
- **Stun Spore: Stun Spore / Sleep Powder / Psychic / Explosion**

### Why It's Good
One of the best sleep leads. Sleep Powder + Explosion makes it a potent team disruptor. Strong Psychic STAB. Can catch Steel-types with HP Fire.

### Counters
Snorlax (huge SpDef), Blissey. Heracross resists Psychic and threatens. Skarmory walls non-HP Fire sets.

name: #094 Gengar [OU]
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] #094 Gengar [OU]

**Role:** Special Attacker / Spinblocker / Exploder  
**Stats:** 60/65/60/110/130/75 (HP/Atk/Def/Spd/SpAtk/SpDef)

### Recommended Sets

- **Standard: Thunderbolt / Ice Punch / Hypnosis / Explosion**
- **Perish Trapper: Mean Look / Perish Song / Protect / Destiny Bond**

### Why It's Good
Ghost-typing blocks Rapid Spin, preserving Spikes. Levitate-like immunity to Ground (Levitate) and Normal immunity. 130 SpAtk hits hard. Hypnosis and Explosion provide disruption.

### Counters
Pursuit Tyranitar traps and KOs. Snorlax tanks special hits. Blissey. Dark-types immune to STAB.

name: #068 Machamp [OU]
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] #068 Machamp [OU]

**Role:** Physical Attacker / Snorlax Counter  
**Stats:** 90/130/80/55/65/85 (HP/Atk/Def/Spd/SpAtk/SpDef)

- **Standard: Cross Chop / Rock Slide / Earthquake / Hidden Power Bug or Fire Blast**
- **RestTalk: Cross Chop / Rock Slide / Rest / Sleep Talk**
- **CurseChamp: Curse / Cross Chop / Rock Slide / Rest**

### Why It's Good
The premier Snorlax counter. Cross Chop 2HKOs most Snorlax. Excellent coverage. Curse sets are potent late-game.

### Counters
Skarmory walls physical attacks. Starmie outspeeds and hits with Psychic/Surf. Gengar immune to Cross Chop.

name: #243 Raikou [OU]
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] #243 Raikou [OU]

**Role:** Special Attacker / RestTalker  
**Stats:** 90/85/75/115/115/100 (HP/Atk/Def/Spd/SpAtk/SpDef)

### Recommended Sets

- **RestTalk: Thunder / Crunch / Rest / Sleep Talk**
- **Offensive: Thunderbolt / Crunch / Hidden Power Ice or Water / Rest**

### Why It's Good
Fastest relevant Electric-type. 115 SpAtk + 115 Speed is devastating. Crunch covers Psychics and Ghosts. RestTalk keeps it healthy.

### Counters
Snorlax (great SpDef), Blissey, Ground-types (immune to Electric), Rhydon, Steelix.

name: #105 Marowak [OU]
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] #105 Marowak [OU]

**Role:** Physical Wallbreaker  
**Stats:** 60/80/110/45/50/80 (HP/Atk/Def/Spd/SpAtk/SpDef)

### Recommended Sets

- **Swords Dance: Swords Dance / Earthquake / Rock Slide / Hidden Power Bug**
- **Standard: Earthquake / Rock Slide / Hidden Power Bug / Rest or Swords Dance**

### Why It's Good
With Thick Club, Marowak has the highest effective Attack in GSC. Earthquake + Rock Slide has perfect neutral coverage. Can 2HKO almost anything after Swords Dance.

### Counters
Skarmory (immune to EQ, resists Rock Slide). Exeggutor. Suicune. Starmie outspeeds. Must be careful of its poor Speed and Special bulk.

name: #242 Blissey [OU]
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] #242 Blissey [OU]

**Role:** Special Wall  
**Stats:** 255/10/10/55/75/135 (HP/Atk/Def/Spd/SpAtk/SpDef)

- **Support: Softboiled / Heal Bell / Toxic / Light Screen or Seismic Toss**

Literally cannot be KOed by special attacks thanks to 255 HP and 135 SpDef. Heal Bell cures team status. Counter punishes physical attackers who try to exploit 10 Defense.

### Counters
Any physical attacker: Machamp, Marowak, Snorlax with Curse. Toxic stalling.

text:

name: Design Origins & Name Etymology
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Design Origins & Name Etymology

### Starters
- **Cyndaquil** — "Cinder" + "quill" (fire hedgehog). Based on the echidna.
- **Totodile** — "Tot" (small) + "crocodile". Baby crocodile design.
- **Chikorita** — "Chicory" (plant) + "-ita" (small, Spanish). Sauropod dinosaur with a leaf.

### Legendary Beasts
- **Raikou** — "Rai" (thunder, Japanese) + "kou" (emperor). Saber-toothed tiger with thunderclouds.
- **Entei** — "En" (flame) + "tei" (emperor). Lion with a volcanic mane.
- **Suicune** — "Sui" (water) + "kune" (from "kunitama," spirit of the land). Aurora borealis mane. Crystal's mascot.

### Legendary Birds
- **Ho-Oh** — "Houou" (phoenix in Japanese). Based on the Fenghuang, a Chinese/Japanese mythological bird.
- **Lugia** — Possibly from Latin "lugeo" (to mourn) or a play on "silver" (its game). Created by Takeshi Shudo for the anime movie first.

### New Type Pokemon
- **Umbreon** — "Umbra" (shadow, Latin) + "eon" (Eevee suffix). Originally intended to be Poison-type, changed to Dark during development.
- **Espeon** — "ESP" (extrasensory perception) + "eon." Cat-like psychic design.
- **Scizor** — "Scissors" + possibly "razor." Red coloring from Metal Coat evolution.
- **Steelix** — "Steel" + "onyx." The original Steel/Ground type.

### Notable Others
- **Wobbuffet** — "That's the way it is!" (Japanese: Sonnansu). Based on a punching bag toy.
- **Smeargle** — "Smear" + "beagle." Painter dog. Sketch copies any move permanently.
- **Unown** — "Unknown." 26 forms representing the Latin alphabet.
- **Shuckle** — "Shuck" (shell) + "turtle." Highest Defense and SpDef base stats possible (230 each) when combined with Power Trick (future gen).
- **Dunsparce** — "Dun" + "dunce" + tsuchinoko (Japanese snake cryptid). One of the most underwhelming Pokemon by design.
- **Delibird** — "Delivery" + "bird." Only move: Present (random damage or healing).
- **Tyrogue** — "Tyro" (beginner) + "rogue." Baby form of the Hitmon family.

---

name: Unique Quirks and Mechanics
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Unique Quirks and Mechanics

### Ditto + Transform
- Ditto transforms into the opposing Pokemon, copying its stats, moves, and type
- In Gen 2, Transform copies the target's DVs (IVs), which affects Shiny status
- Ditto breeding: Ditto can breed with ANY Pokemon that can breed (except other Ditto)
- Ditto is the universal breeding partner — essential for breeding rare males

### Smeargle + Sketch
- Sketch permanently copies the last move used by the opponent
- Can learn ANY move in the game (except Struggle)
- Sketch has 1 PP — use it, then level up to get a new Sketch
- Smeargle can learn Spore (100% accuracy sleep), which is otherwise Paras/Parasect exclusive
- Competitive Smeargle runs Spore + Spikes + Baton Pass (terrifying support set)

### Wobbuffet's Unique Moveset
- Learns only 4 moves ever: Counter, Mirror Coat, Safeguard, Destiny Bond
- Cannot attack directly — only retaliates
- Shadow Tag (ability) traps opponents... but abilities don't exist in Gen 2
- In Gen 2, Wobbuffet is actually not very good — it can't trap opponents

### Unown's Hidden Power
- Unown only knows Hidden Power (can never learn anything else)
- Hidden Power type and base power are determined by DVs
- Different Unown letters have different DV combinations, so different Hidden Power types
- Unown is extremely weak competitively — 336 BST with only one move

### Magikarp's Splash
- Splash does literally nothing (no effect, ever)
- Magikarp can also learn Tackle (Lv15) and Flail (Lv25) in Gen 2
- Despite the memes, Magikarp is not the weakest Pokemon — Sunkern has lower BST (180 vs 200)

### Sunkern — The Weakest Pokemon
- 180 BST — lowest of all 251 Pokemon
- 30 across all stats
- Evolves into Sunflora with Sun Stone (which is... still not very good)

---

name: Shiny Pokemon
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Shiny Pokemon

### Mechanics
- Shiny status determined by DVs (IVs)
- Shiny DVs: Attack DV must be 2, 3, 6, 7, 10, 11, 14, or 15. Def, Speed, and Special DVs must all be exactly 10.
- Probability: 1/8192 (0.012%)
- Shiny Pokemon have sparkle animation when entering battle
- In Gen 2, shininess is visible in the overworld (different palette)

### Guaranteed Shinies
- **Red Gyarados** at Lake of Rage (always Shiny)
- **Odd Egg** from Pokemon Communication Center has 14% Shiny chance (Crystal only)

### Shiny Breeding
- If one parent is Shiny, offspring have a 1/64 chance of being Shiny
- The Red Gyarados is useful as a Shiny breeding parent
- DVs are partially inherited, increasing Shiny odds with Shiny parents

### Shiny-Locked
No Pokemon are "Shiny-locked" in Gen 2. Every encounter (including gifts and legendaries) can be Shiny.

---

name: Hidden/Cut Content
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Hidden/Cut Content

### Beta Pokemon
- **Ruins of Alph** had an unused puzzle room layout (BetaRuinsOfAlphUnsolvedPuzzleRoom.blk)
- Cut trainer classes and dialogue found in data files
- Several unused map warps exist in the ROM

### Debug Features
- Debug menu exists in the code but is inaccessible in retail
- Can be re-enabled via hacking — provides Pokemon/item editors, warp controls
- The debug build had a "walk through walls" flag

### Spaceworld 1997 Demo
- Early beta (leaked 2018) had drastically different Pokemon designs
- Many Gen 2 Pokemon had completely different sprites and typing
- Baby Pokemon like "Kurstraw" (baby Jynx), "Pudi" (baby Growlithe) were cut
- An entire evolution line for Ditto was planned and cut
- Farfetch'd was supposed to have an evolution

### Unused Items
- GS Ball had no event trigger in non-Japanese Crystal until Virtual Console
- Several key items have placeholder descriptions in the code
- The Teru-sama items are leftover debugging placeholder items

---

name: Version Differences (Gold vs Silver vs Crystal)
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Version Differences (Gold vs Silver vs Crystal)

### Crystal-Exclusive Features
- **Animated sprites** — Front sprites animate when Pokemon enters battle
- **Suicune storyline** — Eusine character, static Suicune encounter at Tin Tower
- **Battle Tower** — Olivine City post-game facility
- **Buena's Password** — Daily radio quiz show for points/prizes
- **Move Tutors** — Flamethrower, Thunderbolt, Ice Beam (one each per save)
- **Odd Egg** — From Pokemon Communication Center
- **GS Ball event** — Celebi encounter (Virtual Console worldwide)
- **Expanded Ruins of Alph** — More events and dialogue
- **Character selection** — Can play as female character (Kris)
- **Communication Center** — Mobile System GB features (Japan only originally)

### Wild Pokemon Differences
Pokemon availability varies between versions:

| Pokemon | Gold | Silver | Crystal |

| Vulpix/Ninetales | No | Yes | Yes (Route 36/37) |
| Growlithe/Arcanine | Yes | No | No |
| Mankey/Primeape | Yes | No | No |
| Meowth/Persian | No | Yes | No |
| Spinarak/Ariados | Yes | No | Yes (night) |

| Phanpy/Donphan | No | Yes | Yes (Route 45, morning) |

| Delibird | No | Yes | Yes (Ice Path) |
| Mareep | Yes | Yes | Yes (Route 32, morning/day only in Crystal) |

### Suicune Differences
- **Gold/Silver:** Suicune roams Johto like Raikou and Entei
- **Crystal:** Suicune has a scripted storyline; encountered at specific locations before a static battle at Tin Tower

name: Interesting Battle Mechanics Trivia
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Interesting Battle Mechanics Trivia

### Sleep Mechanics
- Sleep counter is 1-7 turns (randomly chosen when put to sleep)
- In Gen 2, if you switch out a sleeping Pokemon and switch back in, the sleep counter resets — this is a bug
- Rest always puts you to sleep for exactly 2 turns (you wake up on turn 3)

### Critical Hit Quirks
- Gen 2 crit rate is based on Speed stat: base rate = Speed/512
- Focus Energy DOUBLES the crit rate (it was bugged to divide in Gen 1)
- Scope Lens adds +1 stage to crit rate
- Moves like Slash, Crunch, Cross Chop have +1 crit stage

### Confusion Damage
- Confusion self-hit uses a typeless 40-power physical attack
- It uses your own Attack and Defense for damage calculation
- This means high-Attack Pokemon hurt themselves MORE when confused
- DynamicPunch always confuses but only has 50% accuracy

### Type Effectiveness Oddity
- Ghost is super effective against Psychic in Gen 2 (unlike Gen 1 bug where it was ineffective)
- But the only Ghost-type attacking move with decent power is Shadow Ball (80 power, Special)
- Lick (30 power) and Night Shade (fixed damage) are the only other Ghost attacks

### The Dragon Fang Bug
- Dragon Fang is supposed to boost Dragon-type moves by 10%
- Due to a bug, it actually doesn't boost Dragon-type moves at all
- It boosts... nothing. The item is completely useless in Gen 2.

---

name: Famous Players and Strategies
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Famous Players and Strategies

### Top Pokemon by Usage (GSC OU)
1. Snorlax (~90% usage) — On virtually every competitive team
2. Raikou (~35%) — Premier Electric type
3. Zapdos (~30%) — Alternative Electric
4. Skarmory (~25%) — Physical wall + Spikes
5. Starmie (~25%) — Rapid Spin + offense

### Iconic Strategies
- **SkarmBliss** — Skarmory (physical wall) + Blissey (special wall) core. Incredibly annoying to break through.
- **Spikes + Phazing** — Skarmory/Forretress set Spikes, then Roar/Whirlwind forces switches to rack up residual damage.
- **CurseLax** — Snorlax with Curse/Rest/Sleep Talk/Body Slam. The most feared sweeper in GSC.
- **Perish Trapping** — Misdreavus/Gengar with Mean Look + Perish Song. Trap and KO in 3 turns.

### Speed Tiers (Key Thresholds)
| Speed | Pokemon |
|-------|---------|
| 130 | Jolteon, Aerodactyl |
| 120 | Alakazam |
| 115 | Starmie, Raikou |
| 110 | Espeon, Gengar |
| 100 | Zapdos, Charizard, Miltank, Tentacruel, Dragonite (approximate) |
| 95 | Heracross |
| 90 | Machamp, Kangaskhan |
| 85 | Nidoking, Scizor |
| 80 | Dragonite, Marowak |
| 70 | Skarmory, Steelix |

name: Easter Eggs
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Easter Eggs

### Red at Mt. Silver
- The final boss is Red (the protagonist of Gen 1)
- His team: Pikachu Lv81, Espeon Lv73, Snorlax Lv75, Venusaur Lv77, Charizard Lv77, Blastoise Lv77
- Defeating Red rolls credits. The game continues afterward.
- Red does not speak — his only dialogue is "..."

### Professor Oak's Pokemon Talk
- Professor Oak's radio show gives out (mostly useless) Pokemon advice
- Can trigger "Pokemon Music" which plays special remixed music from Gen 1

### Buena's Password (Crystal)
- Daily radio show at 6 PM - midnight
- Listen for the password, then go to the Radio Tower to claim points
- Points exchange for rare items: Rare Candy, Nugget, HP Up, etc.

### Hidden Rocket Admin Imposter
- During the Radio Tower takeover, a Rocket Admin disguises himself as the Radio Tower Director
- If you talk to him in the underground, he reveals himself and battles you

### Trainer House (Viridian City)
- Daily battle against a trainer whose team mirrors the last person you linked/Mystery Gifted with
- If you haven't linked, you fight a default trainer named CAL with:
  Meganium Lv50, Typhlosion Lv50, Feraligatr Lv50

### Mom's Savings
- Your mom saves money from your winnings (if you let her)
- She occasionally buys items for you: Super Potion, Repel, etc.
- She can also buy decorations for your room
- Bug: She sometimes buys useless items at full price

name: Legendary Pokemon
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Legendary Pokemon

### Suicune (Crystal Storyline)

**Location:** Multiple encounters, final battle at Tin Tower
**Level:** 40
**Mechanics:** In Pokemon Crystal (unlike Gold/Silver), Suicune has a unique storyline. You encounter it at:
1. Burned Tower (flees, alongside Raikou and Entei)
2. Cianwood City (cutscene, flees)
3. Route 42 (cutscene, flees)
4. Vermilion City (cutscene, flees)
5. Route 14 (cutscene, flees)
6. **Tin Tower B1F** (static battle, Lv40) — requires Clear Bell + all 3 Legendary Beasts seen

**Catch Rate:** 3 (hardest tier)
**Moves:** Rain Dance, Gust, Aurora Beam, Mist
**Held Item:** None

### Raikou (Roaming)

**Location:** Roaming Johto (after Burned Tower event)
**Level:** 40
**Mechanics:** Appears randomly in Johto grass areas. Flees immediately on encounter. Track via Pokedex after first sighting. Its HP and status persist between encounters.
- Changes route every time you change routes
- Cannot encounter it on water or in caves
- If KOed, it is gone forever (no respawn)

**Catch Rate:** 3
**Moves:** Roar, Thunder Shock, Reflect, Crunch
**Tips:** Use Mean Look/Spider Web to trap, or use a Master Ball. False Swipe + Sleep is optimal.

### Entei (Roaming)

**Location:** Roaming Johto (after Burned Tower event)
**Level:** 40
**Mechanics:** Same roaming mechanics as Raikou.

**Catch Rate:** 3
**Moves:** Roar, Fire Spin, Stomp, Flamethrower
**Tips:** Same strategy as Raikou. Roar will force you out, so use a faster Pokemon with Mean Look.

### Lugia

**Location:** Whirl Islands B2F
**Level:** 60
**Requirements:** Silver Wing (from Radio Tower Director after Team Rocket event)
**Catch Rate:** 3
**Moves:** Aeroblast, Safeguard, Gust, Recover

### Ho-Oh

**Location:** Tin Tower 7F
**Level:** 60
**Requirements:** Rainbow Wing (from Radio Tower Director after Team Rocket event, Crystal only) + Clear Bell
**Catch Rate:** 3
**Moves:** Sacred Fire, Safeguard, Gust, Recover

### Celebi (GS Ball Event)

**Location:** Ilex Forest Shrine
**Level:** 30
**Requirements:** GS Ball (originally Japan-only mobile event; available on Virtual Console worldwide)
**Mechanics:** Take GS Ball to Kurt in Azalea Town. After one day, he returns it. Place it on the shrine in Ilex Forest. Celebi appears.
**Catch Rate:** 45 (much easier than other legendaries)
**Moves:** Leech Seed, Recover, Heal Bell, Safeguard

---

name: Roaming Mechanics (Raikou & Entei)
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Roaming Mechanics (Raikou & Entei)

- Both are released when you fall through the Burned Tower floor
- They roam Johto routes (grass encounters only)
- Each time you enter a new route, they move to a random adjacent route
- If you enter their current route, there's a chance to encounter them in grass
- They always attempt to flee on the first turn (Roar forces you out too)
- HP, status, and catch attempts persist across encounters
- If you KO either one, they disappear permanently
- Using Fly/Teleport to a route they're on works — they don't move when you Fly
- The Pokedex map feature shows their current location after first sighting

name: Overview
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Overview

- **Location:** Olivine City (Crystal only, does not exist in Gold/Silver)
- **Availability:** After entering the Hall of Fame (beating the Elite Four)
- **Format:** Single battles, 3v3 (bring 3 Pokemon)
- **Goal:** Win 7 consecutive battles to complete a streak

---

name: Rules and Restrictions
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Rules and Restrictions

### Team Rules
- You must register exactly 3 Pokemon
- All 3 Pokemon must be different species (no duplicates)
- All 3 Pokemon must hold different items (no duplicate held items)
- Pokemon are NOT healed between battles (items used are consumed)

### Level Groups
The Battle Tower scales to your Pokemon's levels. When you register, your highest-level Pokemon determines the level group:

| Level Group | Your Max Level | Opponent Level |
|-------------|---------------|----------------|
| Level 10 | 1-10 | 10 |
| Level 20 | 11-20 | 20 |
| Level 30 | 21-30 | 30 |
| Level 40 | 31-40 | 40 |
| Level 50 | 41-50 | 50 |
| Level 60 | 51-60 | 60 |
| Level 70 | 61-70 | 70 |
| Level 80 | 71-80 | 80 |
| Level 90 | 81-90 | 90 |
| Level 100 | 91-100 | 100 |

### Banned Pokemon (Ubers Check)
The following Pokemon cannot be entered:
- Mewtwo
- Mew
- Lugia
- Ho-Oh
- Celebi

### Other Restrictions
- Eggs cannot be entered
- You can save and quit mid-streak (resume later)
- If you lose, the streak resets to 0

---

**Easter Eggs:** MASUDA (Junichi Masuda, Pokemon composer), TAJIRI (Satoshi Tajiri, Pokemon creator)

### Pokemon Pool

Each level group has 21 unique pre-built Pokemon (BATTLETOWER_NUM_UNIQUE_MON = 21). Opponents are assigned 3 random Pokemon from this pool.

**Sample Pokemon (Level 10 Group):**

| Pokemon | Held Item | Moves |
|---------|-----------|-------|

All Pokemon have preset stat experience, DVs, moves, and held items. They are fully competitive builds appropriate for their level group.

### Trainer Class Bug (Crystal 1.0)

In Crystal 1.0, there is a bug where the trainer class is not read correctly. Instead, the game uses the 6th character of the trainer's name to determine their sprite/class for dialogue purposes. This was fixed in Crystal 1.1.

name: Battle Mechanics
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Battle Mechanics

### Party Size
- BATTLETOWER_PARTY_LENGTH = 3
- Both player and opponent use exactly 3 Pokemon

### Streak Length
- BATTLETOWER_STREAK_LENGTH = 7
- Win 7 consecutive battles to complete a streak

### Challenge States
The game tracks your challenge state:
1. **BATTLETOWER_NO_CHALLENGE** — Not in a challenge
2. **BATTLETOWER_SAVED_AND_LEFT** — Mid-challenge, saved and exited
3. **BATTLETOWER_CHALLENGE_IN_PROGRESS** — Currently battling
4. **BATTLETOWER_WON_CHALLENGE** — Completed 7 wins
5. **BATTLETOWER_RECEIVED_REWARD** — Collected the prize

### AI Behavior
- Battle Tower opponents use standard trainer AI (no special enhanced AI)
- They use held items effectively (Leftovers for recovery, berries for status cure)
- They have competitive movesets with good type coverage
- No switching AI — they fight with whatever is sent out

---

name: Rewards
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Rewards

After winning 7 consecutive battles, you receive a reward. The reward selection is handled by BATTLETOWERACTION_CHOOSEREWARD and BATTLETOWERACTION_GIVEREWARD.

Possible rewards include:
- **Vitamins:** HP Up, Protein, Iron, Calcium, Carbos
- **Rare items:** Rare Candy
- The specific reward varies by streak number

---

name: Strategy Tips
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Strategy Tips

### Team Building
- Bring 3 Pokemon that cover each other's weaknesses
- Leftovers is the best held item (sustain over 7 battles without healing)
- Recovery moves (Rest, Recover, Milk Drink) are invaluable since you don't heal between battles
- Avoid self-KO moves (Explosion, Self-Destruct) — you need Pokemon alive for 7 battles

### Recommended Pokemon
- **Snorlax** — CurseLax with Rest/Sleep Talk/Body Slam/Curse. Nearly unkillable.
- **Starmie** — Fast, Recover, good coverage with Surf/Thunderbolt/Psychic/Recover
- **Suicune** — Incredibly bulky, RestTalk with Surf/Ice Beam
- **Blissey** — Walls all special attackers, Softboiled for healing, Toxic for damage

### Level Group Strategy
- Level 100 group has the toughest opponents with maxed stats
- Lower level groups can be "gamed" by bringing a strong Lv10 Pokemon
- Level 10 group is surprisingly competitive — opponents have good moves even at low levels
