name: QA Q151: Where can I find the Karate King and Tyrogue?
qa_question: Where can I find the Karate King and Tyrogue?
qa_answer: The Karate King is deep inside Mt. Mortar (Route 42). You need Surf and Waterfall to reach him. He battles you with a team of Fighting-types. Upon defeat, he gives you a Tyrogue (the only way to get one outside of breeding). Tyrogue evolves into Hitmonlee, Hitmonchan, or Hitmontop at Lv20 depending on its Attack vs Defense stats.

name: QA Q156: What are the main differences between Gen 2 and Ge
qa_question: What are the main differences between Gen 2 and Gen 3 mechanics?
qa_answer: Major changes: 1) Abilities introduced (Intimidate, Levitate, etc.). 2) Natures introduced (stat-modifying personalities). 3) EVs replaced stat experience (max 510 total, 252 per stat). 4) IVs replaced DVs (0-31 range instead of 0-15). 5) Physical/Special determined per MOVE instead of per TYPE. 6) Double battles introduced. 7) Fairy type didn't exist until Gen 6.

Category: meta, Difficulty: intermediate

name: QA Q157: Why is the physical/special split by type consider
qa_question: Why is the physical/special split by type considered a design flaw?
qa_answer: Because many Pokemon have mismatched stats and typing. Examples: Gyarados has 125 Attack but Water (Special) STAB — its Water moves use its weaker Special Attack. Sneasel has 95 Attack but Dark (Special) and Ice (Special) — it can't use STAB effectively. Flareon has 130 Attack but Fire (Special). This was fixed in Gen 4 with per-move physical/special categorization.
text:

This was fixed in Gen 4 with per-move physical/special categorization.
Category: meta, Difficulty: intermediate

name: QA Q158: Why is Snorlax not banned in GSC despite being so
qa_question: Why is Snorlax not banned in GSC despite being so dominant?
qa_answer: Although Snorlax has ~95% usage in competitive GSC, it has sufficient counterplay: Machamp, Heracross, Skarmory, and Tyranitar all handle it. The metagame has adapted around Snorlax — teams are built to handle it. Banning Snorlax would fundamentally change the tier and isn't considered necessary because the game is still strategically diverse.

name: QA Q160: What makes Gen 2 competitive unique compared to mo
qa_question: What makes Gen 2 competitive unique compared to modern Pokemon?
qa_answer: Key unique aspects: 1) No abilities — makes certain Pokemon worse/better than in later gens. 2) Leftovers on everything — no choice items (Choice Band came Gen 3). 3) Spikes is the ONLY entry hazard. 4) Small viable Pokemon pool (~25 OU Pokemon). 5) Games are long and defensive. 6) No team preview — surprise factor matters. 7) Explosion is much more viable (higher base power relative to Gen 5+). 8) Baton Pass chains are legal and viable.

name: QA Q161: Why was Celebi banned to Ubers?
qa_question: Why was Celebi banned to Ubers?
qa_answer: Celebi's typing (Grass/Psychic) combined with its 100 base stats across the board, plus its movepool (Heal Bell, Recover, Leech Seed, Baton Pass, Perish Song, Future Sight) makes it too versatile and bulky. It can cleric, stall, pass stats, and attack. Its only event distribution (Japanese Crystal GS Ball) made it extremely rare, but its stats justified the ban regardless.

name: QA Q162: What design philosophy changes happened between Ge
qa_question: What design philosophy changes happened between Gen 1 and Gen 2?
qa_answer: Major shifts: 1) Special stat split into SpAtk/SpDef (Gen 1 had one "Special"). 2) Dark and Steel types added (to balance Psychic dominance from Gen 1). 3) Held items introduced. 4) Breeding introduced. 5) Day/night cycle added. 6) Friendship/happiness system. 7) Shiny Pokemon concept. 8) Two regions in one game. 9) Phone system for trainer rematches. 10) More balanced type chart.

name: QA Q163: What is the significance of the Red battle at Mt.
qa_question: What is the significance of the Red battle at Mt. Silver?
qa_answer: Trainer Red represents the player character from Gen 1 (Red/Blue/Yellow). His team reflects the canonical Gen 1 party: Pikachu (starter in Yellow), the three fully-evolved Kanto starters, plus Espeon and Snorlax. At Lv81 Pikachu and Lv77 others, he is the highest-level trainer in any Pokemon game up to that point. The battle serves as the true final challenge and connects Gen 1 to Gen 2 narratively.

name: QA Q164: Why does Gen 2 have two regions while later games
qa_question: Why does Gen 2 have two regions while later games have only one?
qa_answer: Game Freak originally planned for Gold/Silver to be the final Pokemon games. They wanted to create a definitive experience that concluded the series, so they included both Johto (new) and Kanto (nostalgia). This was possible partly because the Game Boy Color had limited graphics that took less storage space. Later generations had more complex graphics and features that consumed the available ROM/cartridge space, making dual regions impractical.

name: QA Q165: How balanced is Gen 2 compared to Gen 1?
qa_question: How balanced is Gen 2 compared to Gen 1?
qa_answer: Gen 2 is significantly more balanced than Gen 1. Key improvements: 1) Psychic type no longer dominates (Dark type added as counter; Bug and Ghost moves buffed). 2) Special split into SpAtk/SpDef prevents single-stat dominance. 3) Steel type provides defensive balance. 4) More viable competitive Pokemon. 5) Fewer glitches/exploits. 6) Items add strategic depth. Gen 2 still has issues (type-based physical/special split, Snorlax centralization) but is much healthier than Gen 1's Mewtwo/Alakazam meta.

name: QA Q166: What Pokemon were added in Gen 2?
qa_question: What Pokemon were added in Gen 2?
qa_answer: Gen 2 added 100 new Pokemon (#152-#251): the Johto starters (Chikorita line, Cyndaquil line, Totodile line), pre-evolutions of Gen 1 Pokemon (Pichu, Cleffa, Igglybuff, etc.), new evolutions of Gen 1 Pokemon (Crobat, Espeon, Umbreon, Steelix, Scizor, Kingdra, etc.), legendary beasts (Raikou, Entei, Suicune), tower legends (Lugia, Ho-Oh), and Celebi.

name: QA Q167: What are the Gen 2-specific Pokemon types that wer
qa_question: What are the Gen 2-specific Pokemon types that were added?
qa_answer: Gen 2 added two new types: Dark and Steel. Dark was added specifically to counter Psychic (which dominated Gen 1). Dark is immune to Psychic and super-effective against it. Steel was added for defensive balance — it resists 11 types. Notable Dark types: Umbreon, Murkrow, Houndoom, Sneasel, Tyranitar. Notable Steel types: Steelix, Skarmory, Scizor, Forretress, Magneton (retroactively gained Steel).

name: QA Q168: What key items from Gen 2 became staples in future
qa_question: What key items from Gen 2 became staples in future generations?
qa_answer: Key innovations: 1) Held items (carried into all future gens). 2) Breeding system (core mechanic ever since). 3) Shiny Pokemon (became a massive collecting metagame). 4) Day/night system (expanded in every gen). 5) Happiness evolution (became more prevalent). 6) Berries (expanded into Berry farming in Gen 3+). 7) TMs being reusable (introduced in Gen 5, but TMs started here).

name: QA Q169: Why did the competitive community choose Smogon ru
qa_question: Why did the competitive community choose Smogon rules over VGC for Gen 2?
qa_answer: VGC (Video Game Championships) didn't exist until Gen 4 (2009). Gen 2 competitive was entirely fan-organized, primarily through Smogon University, Azure Heights, and GameFAQs. Smogon's tier system (Ubers/OU/UU) and clauses (Sleep Clause, Species Clause, Evasion Clause) became the de facto standard because they created the most balanced and diverse metagame.

name: QA Q170: What is the "Special split" and why did it matter?
qa_question: What is the "Special split" and why did it matter?
qa_answer: In Gen 1, there was a single "Special" stat that governed both Special offense and Special defense. In Gen 2, this was split into Special Attack and Special Defense. This massively changed balance: Pokemon like Chansey (high Gen 1 Special) got high SpDef but low SpAtk. Alakazam kept high SpAtk but got lower SpDef. The split made more Pokemon competitively viable by differentiating offensive and defensive special capabilities.
